attributes.stem:
A 21-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with excessive sweating of her palms that has been present since adolescence. She reports that the sweating is worse with stress and anxiety, causing significant social embarrassment and occupational difficulties with handling papers and using electronic devices. She notes it is not present when sleeping. She has severe anxiety and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears nervous with vital signs within normal limits. Her palms are visibly moist with beads of sweat. The affected skin appears normal with no signs of infection or inflammation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with primary focal hyperhidrosis affecting the palms. Primary hyperhidrosis typically begins in adolescence before 25 years of age and can significantly impact quality of life through social embarrassment and functional impairment. It can be generalised or focal and may be primary, idiopathic or secondary (menopause, hyperthyroidism, propanolol, malaria, or Parkinson disease). Idiopathic focal hyperhidrosis is diagnosed clinically with more than 6 months of bilateral symmetrical sweating, and at least once per week, affecting daily activities, positive family history or that the sweating is not present on sleeping.

Topical aluminium chloride of 20 - 35% is the most appropriate first-line treatment for primary hyperhidrosis. It works by blocking the eccrine sweat gland ducts and is most effective when applied to dry skin at night. This treatment is non-invasive, relatively inexpensive, and has a good safety profile.

Oral glycopyrrolate is a systemic anticholinergic medication that can be effective for hyperhidrosis but is generally reserved for cases that have failed topical therapy due to its systemic side effects including dry mouth, urinary retention, and blurred vision.

Iontophoresis is a second-line treatment that can be effective for palmar hyperhidrosis but requires regular treatments and specialized equipment. It should be considered if topical treatments fail.

Botulinum toxin injections can be effective but are expensive and particularly painful when used in the palms due to the high density of pain receptors. They are typically reserved for cases that have failed conservative management.

Thoracic sympathectomy is a surgical option that should only be considered in severe cases that have failed conservative management, due to the risk of compensatory hyperhidrosis affecting up to 80% of patients.

Additional management strategies include lifestyle modifications such as avoiding triggers, using absorbent powders, and stress management techniques. Patient education about the chronic nature of the condition and its management is essential.
learningPoints: Primary hyperhidrosis commonly affects the palms, axillae, and soles, causing significant social and occupational impairment.
Topical antiperspirants containing aluminium chloride are first-line treatment for primary hyperhidrosis.
Thoracic sympathectomy should be reserved for severe cases that have failed conservative management due to risk of compensatory hyperhidrosis.
references: Sinclair R, Perera E. Hyperhidrosis and bromhidrosis A guide to assessment and management.  Australian Family Physician. 2013: 42 (5).  Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/may/hyperhidrosis-and-bromhidrosis

Loizzi D, Mongiello D, Bevilacqua MT, Raveglia F, Fiorelli A, Congedo MT, Ardò NP, Sollitto F. Surgical management of compensatory sweating: A systematic review. Front Surg. 2023.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10073509/
